summary: Obama proposed a new college ranking system tied to federal funding, based on metrics like affordability, graduation rates, and post-graduation employment. O'Neil argues this will be heavily gamed by administrators and produce strong negative effects without solving the affordability problem. The metrics would pressure colleges to admit students likely to get high-paying jobs, lower academic standards to boost graduation rates, and effectively eliminate liberal arts education. She contends that modeling educational quality is inherently difficult, and the real solution is to invest heavily in state universities while identifying truly fraudulent institutions.
key_points: Obama's proposed ranking system will be gamed just like the US News rankings, but with more severe consequences since it's tied to federal funding
Metrics favoring quick loan repayment and workforce success will push colleges to select for finance and tech-bound students, gutting liberal arts programs
Graduation rate metrics create perverse incentives to simply lower academic standards
The fundamental problem is that educational quality is extremely difficult to model mathematically
The real solution is investing in state schools and making them nearly free, which would force private colleges to compete on cost
money_quotes: So much for liberal arts educations. So much for learning about art, philosophy, or for that matter anything that isn't an easy entrance into the tech or finance sector.
Do we really want to build the college system in this country in the image of Wall Street and Silicon Valley?
Gaming the graduation rate is easy. Just lower your standards for degrees, duh.
Ironically, much of the rise in tuition cost has gone to administrators, whose job it is to game the system for more money.

The mathematical models that already exist and are being proposed are just pathetically bad at it, partly because college, ultimately, isn't only about the facts you learn, or the job you get, or how quickly you get it.
It's actually a life experience which, in the best of cases, enlarges your world view, and gets you to strive for something you might not have known existed before going.
If you really wanted to make costs low, then fund state universities and make them really good, and make them basically free.
key_insight: Any college ranking model tied to funding will be ruthlessly gamed, and metrics favoring economic outcomes will systematically devalue education that doesn't serve Wall Street or Silicon Valley.
